questions.text: What are the four DORA metrics (Lead Time for Changes, Deployment Frequency, Change Failure Rate, Mean Time to Recovery) — their academic origins in Forsgren et al. 'Accelerate' (2018), exact calculation formulas, and what Google's benchmarks define as 'low', 'medium', 'high', and 'elite' performance levels?

key_findings.text: The four DORA metrics are: Deployment Frequency (how often code deploys to production), Lead Time for Changes (time from commit to production deploy), Change Failure Rate (% of deployments causing production failures requiring rollback/hotfix), and Mean Time to Recovery (time to restore service after production failure). Originated from Dr. Nicole Forsgren, Jez Humble, and Gene Kim's 'Accelerate: The Science of Lean Software and DevOps' (2018), based on 6+ years of State of DevOps survey research.

Calculation approaches: Deployment Frequency uses the average (mean) of successful deployments per time period. Lead Time, Change Failure Rate, and MTTR all use the median rather than mean, as median provides a more accurate view of typical performance and is less skewed by outliers.

2024 DORA benchmark levels — Elite: lead time <1 day, deploy on demand, ~5% failure rate, recovery <1 hour (19% of respondents). High: lead time 1 day–1 week, deploy daily–weekly, ~20% failure rate, recovery <1 day (22%). Medium: lead time 1 week–1 month, deploy weekly–monthly, ~10% failure rate, recovery <1 day (35%). Low: remaining performers (25%). These are NOT pre-defined benchmarks — they shift year-to-year based on survey response clustering.

In 2025, DORA moved away from the low/medium/high/elite cluster model entirely, introducing a new framework based on 'archetypes' rather than fixed performance tiers. A fifth metric (reliability) has also been added to the original four.

questions.text: What AI coding benchmarks exist (SWE-bench, HumanEval, MBPP, MultiPL-E, CodeContests, BigCodeBench), how is each scored (pass@k, exact match, execution-based), what are current state-of-the-art results, and how could a multi-agent orchestration system like Aether be evaluated against them?

What academic research exists on evaluating multi-agent AI systems — specifically metrics for task delegation efficiency, inter-agent communication overhead, autonomous decision accuracy, and collective problem-solving effectiveness (e.g., Park et al. generative agents, AutoGen, ChatDev, MetaGPT evaluations)?

What software quality metrics have rigorous published academic or standards-body backing (Halstead complexity, McCabe cyclomatic complexity, Maintainability Index, SQALE, ISO 25010 quality characteristics) — and what published thresholds define 'good' vs 'excellent' code quality?

What empirical software engineering metrics exist for reliability measurement — defect density per KLOC, test coverage effectiveness (mutation testing, branch vs line), MTTR, reliability growth models — with published industry benchmarks from IEEE, ISO, or academic sources?

How could Aether instrument its existing data sources (580+ tests across 64 test files, 24 agents with structured JSON returns, pheromone signal lifecycle, midden failure tracking, colony state phase transitions, build phase timing, learning/instinct promotion pipeline) to concretely calculate the metrics identified in q1-q5?

What presentation frameworks and maturity models exist for communicating development progress to non-technical sponsors (Capability Maturity Model CMMI, Technology Readiness Levels TRL, OKR-based dashboards, GitHub's 'readme stats' patterns) — which would best serve an open-source AI tool website seeking donations?
